feedback.text: The online space is broken. Corporations are violating people's privacy by selling our data to anyone willing to pay, they steal our creative output to feed it into A.I. training datasets, and they deliberately leverage user-hostile design to trap people in their extortionist ecosystems. It doesn't have to be this way, though. The one thing these corporations fear more than anything else is government regulation that reflects the will of the people. Corporate leadership knows that once the people understand the game that's being played, it'll be game over for the crooked businesspeople who got us into this mess. Speaking of games, one example of unfairness in the online space that this act should address is the issue of video game publishers making games inaccessible after people have purchased them. Many games, even ones that do not have an online play component, have been effectively killed by their publishers, leaving millions of customers with a defective product that no longer works. This would be unacceptable in any other industry, and gaming should not be an exception. Imagine if you buy a new dinner table. You like this table. Your family has a lovely dinner at this table every evening. Then one day, the company that makes the table barges in and removes the table's surface, leaving you only with the legs. They don't reimburse you, but they also threaten you with serious legal action if you add a new tabletop to those useless legs you're left with. If this sounds stupid and even Kafkaesque, congratulations, you now understand why the Stop Killing Games campaign already has the support of millions of EU citizens! Digital fairness is a noble goal to strive for, and I applaud this initiative. I feel strongly that protecting video games and other digital media by forcing publishers to provide common-sense End Of Life (EOL) support for their products through effective regulation should be included in this act. Thank you for your consideration.
